title: Insecure Communication Channels (Man-in-the-Middle Exposure)
description: Learn how insecure communication channels allow attackers to intercept, alter, or steal data exchanged between two parties.

attack_stages.title: Data Transmission
attack_stages.description: Two systems communicate over a network to exchange sensitive information.

attack_stages.title: Unsecured Communication Channel
attack_stages.description: The communication is not fully encrypted or protected against interception.

attack_stages.title: Traffic Interception
attack_stages.description: An attacker secretly intercepts data between the two communicating parties.

attack_stages.title: Man-in-the-Middle Attack
attack_stages.description: The attacker positions themselves between both parties to observe or modify data in transit.

attack_stages.title: Data Manipulation
attack_stages.description: The attacker alters, injects, or steals sensitive information during transmission.

prevention_points.title: Use Encryption (TLS/HTTPS)
prevention_points.description: Encrypt all communications using HTTPS/TLS to prevent data interception.

prevention_points.title: Use Secure Networks
prevention_points.description: Avoid unsecured public Wi-Fi or require VPN connections for remote access.

prevention_points.title: Prevent DNS Spoofing
prevention_points.description: Use DNSSEC and trusted resolvers to ensure correct domain resolution.

prevention_points.title: Secure Communication Channels
prevention_points.description: Use VPNs and end-to-end encryption to protect data integrity and confidentiality.

nodes.title: Scenario
nodes.text: Two systems communicate over a network to exchange sensitive data such as login credentials, financial information, or personal records.

nodes.title: What is a Man-in-the-Middle Attack?
nodes.text: A Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attack happens when an attacker secretly intercepts communication between two parties without their knowledge.

nodes.title: Insecure Communication Channels
nodes.text: When communication is not properly encrypted or secured, attackers can intercept and manipulate data in transit. This can happen on public Wi-Fi, misconfigured networks, or systems without proper encryption.

nodes.title: Attacker Goal
nodes.text: The attacker wants to secretly intercept communication between two parties to steal sensitive information or manipulate data without being detected.

nodes.title: How can attackers perform a Man-in-the-Middle attack?

choices.text: By intercepting unencrypted data on insecure networks like public Wi-Fi.
By physically breaking into the server room.
By deleting firewall rules from inside the system.

nodes.title: Try Again
nodes.text: MitM attacks typically rely on intercepting or redirecting network traffic rather than physical access or direct system modification.

nodes.title: Man-in-the-Middle Attack in Progress
nodes.text: The attacker successfully positions themselves between two communicating systems and begins intercepting data such as login credentials, session tokens, or financial transactions.

nodes.title: Impact
nodes.text: MitM attacks can lead to stolen credentials, unauthorized account access, malware injection, and manipulation of sensitive communications such as financial transactions.

nodes.title: How to Prevent This
nodes.text: Organizations should enforce HTTPS/TLS encryption for all communications, use VPNs for remote access, avoid unsecured public Wi-Fi for sensitive operations, and implement DNS security protections such as DNSSEC to prevent spoofing attacks.

nodes.title: Attack Replay
nodes.text: Review how an attacker intercepted network traffic and positioned themselves between two communicating parties to steal or manipulate data.

nodes.title: Scenario Complete
nodes.text: Insecure communication channels allow attackers to intercept and manipulate data in transit. Encryption and secure communication protocols are essential to prevent Man-in-the-Middle attacks.
